items.title: Bias–variance / underfitting vs overfitting
items.description: Underfitting and overfitting are the main reasons generalization fails — high error everywhere vs low train error with high test error. This topic defines the target (unseen-data performance); bias–variance explains the trade-off mechanics.

items.title: Cross-validation
items.description: k-fold and related schemes repeatedly hold out labeled folds to estimate generalization more reliably than a single train/test split — the standard procedure for measuring unseen-data performance during model selection.

items.title: Early stopping
items.description: Stopping training when validation error stops improving prevents further overfitting on training noise — a practical technique to close the generalization gap without changing the definition of generalization itself.

items.title: Training and inference
items.description: Generalization is estimated by fitting on train data then running predict (inference) on held-out labeled rows — the same forward-only scoring pattern used in production, with labels available only for evaluation.

items.title: Train / validation / test splits
items.description: Hold-out validation and test sets supply the unseen labeled data on which generalization is estimated before deployment — without mixing those rows into fit().

items.title: Machine learning
items.description: The field's practical success criterion is useful behavior on new data, not training-set perfection — generalization is the central ML performance concept operationalized in every supervised pipeline.
